steps.title: Identify the key signal
steps.content:
The passage explains that longitudinal studies are expensive because they span many years, and this time span enables them to reveal causal patterns.

It then contrasts this with public opinion studies that don't need to find cause-and-effect, stating they “can succeed without longitudinal methods.” The decisive clue is this conditional: if a study doesn't require the time-intensive longitudinal approach, it doesn't need the expense associated with it.

steps.title: Apply the logic to find the consequence
steps.content:
If the public opinion study doesn't need the multi-year longitudinal methods that make NILS expensive, it should cost considerably less to complete.

The other choices either swap necessity for suitability, reverse the cost direction, or add reliability claims the passage never makes.

This matches choice A — A is correct.
